fed_monetary: The FOMC holds the federal funds rate at 3.50–3.75% for a second consecutive meeting, with St. Louis Fed President Musalem stating on April 1 that 'the current setting of the policy rate will remain appropriate' while flagging a highly uncertain 2026 outlook. Powell has publicly dismissed stagflation comparisons, noting unemployment near its long-run normal and inflation only ~1 percentage point above target, yet simultaneously acknowledged rising dual-mandate tensions stemming from tariff-driven price pressure. J.P. Morgan's chief U.S. economist concludes that 'the Fed's next move will not happen any time soon,' with the policy bias tilted toward holding as core PCE prints at 3.06%—26 bps above the Fed's own estimate.
labor_data: March 2026 nonfarm payrolls surged +178K, nearly doubling the +100K consensus, while the unemployment rate held at 4.3% in line with estimates, confirming a labor market that is resilient but no longer tightening. Weekly initial claims of 202K came in below the 210K estimate, signaling limited near-term deterioration in hiring conditions. Tariff-driven policy uncertainty—described by KPMG as having caused firms to 'shelve major investment decisions, notably employment'—represents the primary tail risk to the current benign labor picture heading into Q2.
fiscal_supply: Treasury's Q1–Q2 FY2026 guidance projects $683 billion in net borrowing across the January–June window, with the portfolio remaining skewed toward short-duration issuances—4-week bill auctions now averaging $101 billion per offering versus a 2016 average of $47 billion. Treasury has signaled it is 'preliminarily considering future increases to nominal coupon and FRN auction sizes,' a meaningful longer-end supply overhang that competes directly with the 10-year at 4.34%. The TGA balance stands at $847.7B and RRP has collapsed to $15.3B, leaving reserves the dominant liquidity buffer at $3,026.7B; the next quarterly refunding statement is due May 4.
corporate: Q1 2026 earnings season opens with S&P 500 consensus EPS growth tracking at 13.2% YoY—above the 12.8% estimate set at year-end and on pace for a sixth consecutive quarter of double-digit growth—providing a fundamental floor under the SPX at 6,783 (+2.5% session). HY OAS of 312 bps and IG OAS of 108 bps remain historically compressed, reflecting accommodative financial conditions despite a composite liquidity score at only the 31st percentile. Elevated valuations (forward price-to-FCF at 27.4x, 37% above the 20-year average) and WTI crude at $94.87 create margin-compression risk, particularly in energy-intensive sectors, as the Iran conflict's Strait of Hormuz disruption is characterized by the IEA as the 'largest supply disruption in the history of the global oil market.'
next_catalyst: The March 2026 CPI print, scheduled for Friday April 10 at 8:30 a.m. ET, is the single highest-stakes release of the week; with February CPI at 2.66% (vs. 2.4% estimate) and core CPI at 2.73%, a third consecutive upside surprise would materially reset rate-cut timing expectations and pressure the long end of the curve.
sources: Fed Holds Rates at 3.5–3.75%; Musalem Says Policy 'Well Positioned' — St. Louis Fed / AEI Remarks, April 1, 2026
